title: Anatomy of Java 🦴
description: Break down classes, methods, and statements.

slides.title: The Java Russian Doll 🪆
slides.content:
In the last lesson, we learned `System.out.println("Hello World");`.

But in Java, you can't just throw that code into a file and click run. Java is very strict and organized. Your code needs to live inside "containers".

Think of it like Russian nesting dolls.

slides.title: The Biggest Doll: Classes 📦

quizData.question: In Java, what is the outermost container that all your code must live inside?
quizData.explanation: Every single line of Java code that can run must be wrapped inside a Class.

slides.title: The Medium Doll: Methods ⚙️
slides.content:
## Methods

Inside a Class, we have smaller containers called **Methods**.

A method is a collection of instructions that are grouped together to perform an operation. Java looks for a very specific method to start running your program. It's called the `main` method.

slides.title: The main() Method 🚦

quizData.question: Which method does the Java runtime look for to start executing your code?
quizData.explanation: The main() method is always the entry point of any Java application.

slides.title: The Smallest Doll: Statements 📜
slides.content:
## Statements

Statements are the actual instructions you give to in Java. 
`System.out.println("Hello");` is a statement!

All statements live inside the methods.

slides.title: Putting It All Together 🧩
slides.content:
Let's assemble our Russian Doll. Class -> Method -> Statement.
This is the absolute minimum code required to print Hello World in Java.

quizData.question: Which of the following describes the correct nesting order in Java code?
quizData.explanation: A Statement goes inside a Method, and a Method goes inside a Class!
